"Not your keys — not your coins": how self-custody actually works

When people say "I have bitcoin in my wallet," that's not quite accurate. The coins never leave the blockchain — they are always recorded in the distributed ledger. What sits in the wallet is something else: the private key, that is, the right to control the record. Whoever holds the key owns the money. Everything else is just an interface.

Cryptocurrency doesn't sit in a wallet — it is recorded on the blockchain. The wallet only stores the key. We break down what that means in practice and why a single mistake when copying an address can cost you everything.

When people say "I have bitcoin in my wallet," that's not quite accurate. The coins never leave the blockchain — they are always recorded in the distributed ledger. What sits in the wallet is something else: the
, that is, the right to control the record. Whoever holds the key owns the money. Everything else is just an interface.
The seed phrase IS the money

The twelve or twenty-four words a wallet asks you to write down at creation are not a "backup copy just in case." They are the mathematical root from which all of your private keys are derived. The consequences are simple and unforgiving:
Anyone who has seen the phrase has gained full access. Forever: you can't change it "like a password" — you can only move the money to a new wallet.
Lost the phrase and the device — the money is still on the blockchain, but no one can control it anymore. There is no support service that will "restore access."
A phrase photographed on your phone or saved in notes with cloud sync is no longer a secret.
Self-custody is a trade: full control in exchange for full responsibility. The bank will no longer make mistakes on your behalf — but it won't save you either.

The most underrated risk isn't hackers or a "blockchain hack" — it's carelessness when copying an address. There is an entire class of attacks — transaction history poisoning (address poisoning). The scheme works like this: an attacker sees your transfers in the public ledger and sends you a tiny transaction from an address that
begins and ends with the same characters
as the address of your real counterparty.

The bet is on habit: people check the first and last four characters, but never the middle. Later, the person copies the address "from history," sends a large amount there — and it goes to the look-alike. Blockchain transactions are irreversible: no one can cancel a transfer.
The rules that cover almost everything
Take the recipient's address only from the original source — a direct message from your counterparty, an official website, your own records. Never from transaction history.
Verify the address in full, or at least by several characters in the
For a large transfer — send a test amount first, then the main one.
Store the seed phrase on paper or metal, offline, in two geographically separate locations.
For amounts whose loss would be painful — a hardware wallet: it signs transactions inside itself, and the key never touches the computer.

The honest answer: not for everyone and not always. If the amount is small and your experience is limited, a regulated exchange with two-factor authentication may be safer than a seed phrase in your phone's notes. Self-custody becomes mandatory once the amount becomes significant — and by that point, the habits from the list above should already be running on autopilot.
This is educational material, not financial advice. Verify any actions involving your money independently.
